planting.note: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun in early spring or fall; perennial blanket flower requires excellent drainage and actually blooms more abundantly in lean soils than in rich, moist ones. It is the parent species of the hybrid Gaillardia x grandiflora and is hardier and longer-lived in harsh conditions than the hybrids. Direct seeding is preferable as the taproot resents disturbance.

directions: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; perennial blanket flower is the native wildflower parent of the hybrid blanket flowers sold in garden centers, native to the open, dry grasslands, prairies, and meadows of the Rocky Mountains and Great Plains from British Columbia to Colorado and Nebraska. The flowers are large, vivid daisy-like composites with bright-yellow ray petals banded at the base with orange-red, surrounding a prominent, rounded, dark-orange to reddish central disk — a flame-like, sunset color combination that is unmistakably blanket flower. As the true perennial species (unlike annual G. pulchella), it reliably returns each year from deep taproots and, given excellent drainage and lean soil, can persist for many years. It is more cold-hardy and drought-tolerant than the hybrid G. x grandiflora and succeeds in difficult, dry positions where the hybrids fail. Blooms continuously from June through frost in favorable conditions, providing months of vivid color for butterflies, bees, and other pollinators.

planting.note: Sow seeds in late fall or early spring in lean, very well-drained, neutral to alkaline, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; Ozark sundrops develops a deep taproot that makes transplanting difficult after the first season — direct seeding or planting very small container plants while young is preferable. It is extremely drought-tolerant once established and thrives in the dry, alkaline soils of limestone-based gardens.

directions: Sow seeds or plant small container plants in lean, very well-drained, neutral to alkaline, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; Ozark sundrops produces the largest flowers of any Oenothera — enormous, four-petaled, silky-bright-yellow flowers reaching 4-5 inches across that open in the evening and often remain open through the morning of the following day, giving them more garden visibility than most evening primroses. The large, bowl-shaped flowers glow with an almost luminous yellow in the low-angle evening light. The plant itself is low-growing and spreading, typically only 6-12 inches tall, with narrow, lance-shaped leaves that form a mat-like groundcover decorated with these spectacular flowers from May through July. After flowering, the plant produces distinctive large, winged seed pods (macrocarpa = large fruit) that are ornamental when mature. Native to the dry, limestone and chalk prairies of the Ozarks and central Great Plains, it is exceptionally drought-tolerant and long-lived in well-drained, alkaline soils with minimal supplemental watering once established.

planting.note: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, sandy soil in full sun; large-flowered penstemon requires excellent drainage and fails in heavy clay or consistently moist soils. Stratify seeds in cold (refrigerator) for 30-60 days to improve germination. Established plants are extremely drought-tolerant and are best left undisturbed as the deep taproot makes division difficult. Short-lived (2-4 years) but self-sows in appropriate conditions.

directions: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; large-flowered penstemon bears the largest individual flowers of any native North American penstemon — the spectacular, tubular, inflated flowers are lavender-pink to pale violet-pink, up to 1.5 inches long, and carried in dense whorls along tall, upright stems that are very beautiful from May through June. The outstanding foliage is also a distinguishing feature: large, rounded to egg-shaped, intensely glaucous (blue-green, waxy) leaves that are clasped to the stem in pairs and provide exceptional cool blue-green color throughout the growing season even when not in flower. Native to dry, sandy prairies of the central Great Plains from South Dakota and Iowa south to Nebraska and Kansas, it thrives in lean, alkaline, sandy soils and develops a deep taproot that makes it drought-tolerant once established. An important early nectar source for bumblebees, sphinx moths, and ruby-throated hummingbirds. The beautiful blue-green winter foliage rosette is ornamental even in cold months.

planting.note: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, neutral to alkaline, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; dotted blazing star is extraordinarily drought-tolerant, developing a deep corm-like root system that enables plants to survive extreme drought conditions. It grows slowly in early years as it develops its deep root system before investing in aerial growth. Established plants can live for 100 or more years in undisturbed prairie conditions.

directions: Sow seeds in lean, very well-drained, neutral to alkaline, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; dotted blazing star is the most drought-tolerant blazing star species, native to the dry, sandy and gravelly prairies and rocky hillsides of the central Great Plains from Canada to Texas and New Mexico. It is distinguished from other liatris by its very narrow, almost needle-like leaves dotted with small, translucent oil glands (the punctata of the name) and its compact, lower stature compared to the common spike gayfeather. The purple flower spikes open from top to bottom in August and September — the characteristic reverse bloom order of all liatris — providing late-season color and an important nectar source for monarchs and other migrating butterflies. The deep corm-like root system enables plants to survive extreme drought and poor soils, and established plants can live for 100 or more years in undisturbed prairie conditions — one of the longest-lived prairie wildflowers. Slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall for winter or early spring germination in lean, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; purple viper's bugloss is a cool-season annual that germinates in autumn or early spring and flowers in late spring. CAUTION: It is classified as a noxious weed and invasive species in California, Oregon, several other western states, and Australia, where it spreads aggressively in rangelands and is toxic to livestock due to pyrrolizidine alkaloid content. Confirm it is legal and non-invasive in your region before planting.

directions: Sow seeds in fall in lean, well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; purple viper's bugloss is a Mediterranean annual and biennial wildflower producing masses of brilliantly colored flowers that cycle through a remarkable color change — opening pink in bud, maturing to vivid violet-blue to purple, and sometimes to bright blue or pure pink, creating multicolored mixed clusters on a single plant. The flower color is one of the most intense and vivid purples available in an annual wildflower. It is an extraordinary pollinator plant for bees and butterflies throughout its spring bloom period. As a meadow flower, it has been incorporated into many wildflower seed mixes for its ornamental value. However, purple viper's bugloss has become invasive in many parts of Australia (where it is called Paterson's Curse after the Paterson family on whose property it first appeared in the 1880s), California, and other regions with a Mediterranean climate, escaping cultivation to dominate rangeland and pasture. It contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids toxic to horses and cattle. Always verify local legal status before planting.
